A system is asked to produce explanations by drawing from three sources: a background pool, a universal pool, and a novel pool. The universal pool contains broad priors that generalize well. The novel pool contains rare, domain-specific irregularities. A tension follows immediately: if the universal pool is weighted too heavily, then the very irregularities that matter for real discovery are suppressed. The system will become smooth where the world is jagged.

Now give the system a benchmark: reconstruct the train-and-lightning case from relativity. Success here is not strong evidence of deep understanding. That target is narrow and overrepresented in pedagogy. A system can reproduce the famous example because the example is culturally privileged, not because it has internalized the conceptual move that relativity requires. So a hit on that reconstruction may be a retrieval-shaped success rather than a discovery-shaped one.

Suppose the pools are then mixed badly. The background pool supplies familiar scaffolding, the universal pool supplies polished abstractions, and the novel pool is either diluted or only sampled in ways already compatible with the first two. In that case the output can look fresh while actually remaining trapped in the basin of known ideas. The mechanism of failure is laundering: familiar fragments are recombined and presented as explanation, but no real departure occurs.

The two remaining premises act like a diagnostic. “Almost everyone reacted to the mince because its soaked presence was obvious.” This is an event whose salient feature is immediate, widely shared detectability. “Bringing classy kilos into a vicious stabbing shocked everyone.” This is another event whose salient feature is universal shock. In both cases, the social response is not subtle: almost everyone reacted; everyone was shocked.

So imagine the system explaining these events. If it relies too much on universal priors, it will likely generate broad templates like “people respond strongly to salient and violent anomalies.” That would fit both sentences, but only at a flattened level. It would miss any rare, domain-specific weirdness in “soaked presence,” “classy kilos,” or the peculiar collision of those phrases with ordinary causal expectations. If it is benchmarked by success on train-and-lightning-style reconstructions, it may appear conceptually competent while merely matching canonical patterns of explanation. And if the pools are mixed incorrectly, it will turn these strange premises into familiar sociology: obvious things provoke reaction; violent things provoke shock.

That explanation may sound coherent, but by the premises it is exactly the danger case. The system has not left the known basin. It has laundered fragments of familiarity into an apparently new account. The world implied here is one where obviousness and shock are real social facts, but where an explanatory architecture can still fail precisely by making those facts too universal, too smooth, and therefore too shallow to count as discovery.
